section_title: Tidal Pattern: One Spike vs. Two Bulges

fingerprint_1.name: monitor.py hardcodes M2 = LUNAR_H / 2 — the "/2" divisor is Newton's two-bulge tidal theory on a sphere. The dome's own code builds in the two-bulge assumption that SEC-2.2 argues its geometry cannot produce.
fingerprint_1.kernel_of_truth: The dome author is correct that M2 = 12.4206 h is very close to half of 24.84 h, and that any model running on a ~24.84 h lunar cycle can "produce" M2 by arithmetic. The section already flags this as tautological. What the section does not yet say is that the dome's own verification script treats this as a first-principles derivation — giving the appearance of a physical prediction — and in doing so silently inherits the exact assumption (two bulges per lunar day) that SEC-2.2 shows the geometry cannot support.
fingerprint_1.but_here_is_why_it_fails: In monitor.py (lines 441–452), the M2 domain check is literally `m2_pred = LUNAR_H / 2` with the formula string "M2 = lunar_circuit/2 = 24.84/2". The divisor 2 has a specific origin in tidal theory: the Newtonian tidal potential on a sphere expands as a second-rank spherical harmonic, U ∝ P₂(cos θ) = (3cos²θ − 1)/2, which places TWO bulges per body rotation. The tide therefore has half the lunar-day period — the factor of 2 is the degree of the spherical harmonic. On the dome, a local moon at 2,534 km altitude does not produce a cos(2θ) pattern at all: the tidal force field is a single-lobed spike directly beneath the moon (as SEC-2.2 demonstrates quantitatively: 48% of peak at 2,000 km offset, 9% at 5,000 km, 0.6% at equator distance, 0.2% at the rim). The natural tidal period from dome geometry is one cycle per lunar pass, not half. The dome's code assumes the answer it is supposed to derive. This converts SEC-2.2 from a purely geometric argument ("nearby moon gives one spike") into a dome-vs-dome contradiction ("nearby moon gives one spike AND the dome's own verification script assumes two bulges"). A reader who accepts the geometry argument now also has the dome's source code as internal evidence.

globe_value.name: Second-rank spherical harmonic degree in Newtonian tidal theory
globe_value.derivation: The Earth-Moon tidal potential on a sphere of radius R at distance d expands as U_tidal ≈ (GM_moon/d) · (R/d)² · P₂(cos θ), where P₂ is the Legendre polynomial of degree ℓ=2. The ℓ=2 symmetry places two equal maxima per body rotation (at θ = 0 and θ = π), giving the "divide by 2" that produces M2 from the lunar day.
globe_value.source: Standard tidal theory — Doodson 1921; Cartwright & Tayler 1971; Munk & Cartwright 1966. The ℓ=2 structure requires a distant perturber (d ≫ R) and a spherical perturbed body.

dome_value.name: m2_pred = LUNAR_H / 2 in monitor.py
dome_value.derivation: LUNAR_H = 24.84 is hardcoded as an ECM constant (line 218) under the "LOCKED" header. The M2 domain check (lines 441–452) computes m2_pred = LUNAR_H / 2 = 12.42 h and compares to m2_obs = 12.4206 h, reporting error_pct ≈ 0.0048%.

quantitative_link.source_of_factor_2: P₂(cos θ) spherical-harmonic tidal potential on a sphere — ℓ=2 gives two bulges per rotation
quantitative_link.dome_geometry_consequence: Tidal force at offset from sub-lunar point (moon at 2,534 km over disc): 48% at 2,000 km, 9% at 5,000 km, 0.6% at 14,000 km, 0.2% at 20,015 km — single-lobed, no antipodal bulge. The natural period is one cycle per lunar pass (≈ 24.84 h), not 12.42 h.
quantitative_link.internal_contradiction: monitor.py's M2 check is only correct if the tidal force field has ℓ=2 symmetry. SEC-2.2 proves the dome's geometry does not produce ℓ=2 symmetry. Therefore monitor.py's M2 check is self-refuting: it confirms a globe-theory prediction, not a dome prediction.

verified_code_excerpts: Line 591: "formula": "S2 = solar_circuit/2 = 24.00/2" (same /2 pattern for solar tides)
code_evidence.notes: LUNAR_H is a hardcoded literal under the "ECM CONSTANTS (LOCKED)" header. No script in the repo derives 24.84 h from dome geometry (disc radius, moon altitude, firmament height). No script in the repo computes why the period divisor is 2 rather than 1 — the /2 is written into the formula string as if axiomatic. The S2 solar-tidal check has the same structure (SOLAR_H / 2), implying the same spherical-harmonic inheritance for solar tides.
code_evidence.verification_method: GitHub API read of john09289/predictions monitor.py, 2026-04-15

fingerprint_1.why_it_matters: SEC-2.2 currently refutes the tidal WINs on geometric grounds: a local moon produces one spike, not two bulges. This fingerprint adds a second, independent line of attack: the dome's own verification script assumes two bulges (by dividing by 2) while its geometry would produce one spike. A dome defender can no longer retreat to "the M2 timing is a pure kinematic prediction that doesn't depend on spatial pattern" — the /2 divisor IS the spatial pattern, in equation form. The simultaneous commitment to (a) one-lobed gravitational geometry and (b) /2-derived M2 timing is a logical contradiction present in the dome code itself, independent of any external observation.

defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim: The /2 is just empirical — the dome fitted the formula to observations, and it happens to match.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter: If /2 is purely empirical, then M2 is not a prediction — it is a retrodiction, at which point the 67/71 "confirmed predictions" tally cannot count M2 as one. The dome site does count it (WIN-044). To claim predictive status, the /2 must be derived; to escape the contradiction, it must be admitted as a fit. The dome cannot have both.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim_2: A nearby moon on a disc could still produce two tidal events per lunar day if the disc rotates under the moon — one when the moon rises, one when it sets.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter_2: That would give two UNEQUAL events separated by a short interval (the time the moon is "up"), not two equal events separated by 12.42 h. The observed M2 is a sinusoidal ℓ=2 pattern with equal amplitude at θ = 0 and θ = π (same latitude on opposite sides of a sphere, simultaneously at high tide). A rising/setting pattern under a local moon would produce (a) two tides only in locations directly under the moon's path, (b) no tide at points away from the track, and (c) variable interval depending on moon altitude — none of which matches observation. Moreover, monitor.py's /2 gives a single period (12.42 h), not an interval pattern.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim_3: The formula "M2 = lunar_circuit/2" might be shorthand for something more nuanced that isn't in the public code.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter_3: The dome author's timestamping system (status_history.json OTS anchors) covers the results of monitor.py. The public repo IS the verification system. If a private derivation exists, it cannot participate in the timestamp chain or the 67/71 tally — both of which are built from monitor.py's output. The claim "there's secret code that justifies this" severs the dome's own provenance apparatus from its predictions.

fingerprint_2.name: All three dome time constants (LUNAR_H, SOLAR_H, SIDEREAL) are globe-rotation observables, hardcoded "LOCKED" without derivation from dome geometry. The dome has no native definition of a "day" — it transcribes the sphere's definition.
fingerprint_2.kernel_of_truth: Dome astronomy must produce periodicities that match observation — sunrise, sunset, lunar cycles are empirically fixed, so any model has to reproduce 24 h and 24.84 h to be non-ridiculous. Getting these values right is a prerequisite, not an achievement. The author is right that a dome-moon on some circuit could in principle produce a 24.84 h apparent period — geometry admits that.
fingerprint_2.but_here_is_why_it_fails: The dome does not DERIVE these periods; it adopts them. In monitor.py lines 218–220, under a "LOCKED" banner: LUNAR_H = 24.84, SOLAR_H = 24.00, SIDEREAL = 23.9345. All three are textbook globe-frame constants. SIDEREAL = 23.9345 h is the rotation period of a sphere relative to the fixed stars — a quantity whose very definition requires a 3D rotating body embedded in an inertial frame (how do you define "fixed stars" on a flat disc with the celestial sphere replaced by a firmament 9,086 km up?). SOLAR_H = 24.00 h is the mean solar day, defined by the identity 1/SOLAR_H = 1/SIDEREAL − 1/year (subtracting the orbital rate to convert sidereal to synodic) — again, a sphere-plus-orbit two-body definition. LUNAR_H = 24.84 h is the lunar day, derived by the analogous identity 1/LUNAR_H = 1/SIDEREAL − 1/lunar_sidereal_month — requiring both the sphere's rotation rate and the moon's inertial-frame orbital rate. On the dome, "orbital rate" has no native meaning: the moon traces a circuit at 2,534 km altitude, and its apparent period depends on the disc's rotation relative to the firmament AND the moon's circuit rate — two free parameters the dome never specifies. The dome's code simply imports the globe's answer.

globe_value.name: Globe rotation / orbital periods
globe_value.derivation: SIDEREAL (23.9345 h): Earth rotation period measured against distant inertial reference; requires sphere + fixed stars. SOLAR_H (24.00 h by definition of the hour): mean solar day = 1/(1/SIDEREAL − 1/365.2564 d). LUNAR_H (24.84 h): mean lunar day = 1/(1/SIDEREAL − 1/27.3217 d), where 27.3217 d is the sidereal lunar month.
globe_value.source: IERS standards; standard spherical astronomy (Meeus, Astronomical Algorithms; Seidelmann, Explanatory Supplement).

dome_value.name: "LOCKED" ECM constants
dome_value.dome_derivation_present: None. No script computes these from disc radius, firmament height, moon altitude, or sun altitude.

quantitative_link.sidereal_definition_on_dome: Requires specifying (a) disc rotation rate about its own axis and (b) a "fixed-star frame" against which to measure. The dome has no fixed-star frame — the firmament itself is a rotating 9,086-km-high ceiling with attached stars, making 'sidereal' circular.
quantitative_link.lunar_day_definition_on_dome: Requires 1/LUNAR_H = 1/(disc-rotation-period) − 1/(moon-circuit-period). Neither the disc rotation period nor the moon circuit period appears as an independent dome parameter. The dome sets LUNAR_H = 24.84 directly — which is backwards: the lunar day should be the output, not an input.
quantitative_link.internal_contradiction: The dome criticizes globe astronomy elsewhere as "curve fitting to assumed geometry," yet its own time constants are globe observables hardcoded without dome-side derivation. K1 Tidal Period domain (monitor.py lines 454–462) is even more explicit: "K1 = sidereal_day = 23.9345 h" with "predicted" = SIDEREAL and "error_pct" = 0.0 — the dome reports an exact pass on a value it already set equal to the observation.

code_evidence.notes: The K1 domain is the cleanest example: predicted = SIDEREAL and observed = 23.9345 — the same number written twice. The "0.0% error" is a tautological report. This is the globe's sidereal day passed through as a "prediction."
code_evidence.verification_method: GitHub API read of john09289/predictions monitor.py, 2026-04-15

fingerprint_2.why_it_matters: SEC-2.2 focuses on spatial pattern (one spike vs two bulges). This fingerprint extends the argument to the temporal side: even before the /2 divisor problem, the RAW inputs (24.84 h, 24.00 h, 23.9345 h) are not dome-derived quantities. Combined with fingerprint 1, the full picture is: the dome imports three globe-rotation constants, divides one by 2 using globe-tidal-theory logic, and reports the result as a prediction. Every numerical step is globe physics wearing dome labels. This is the cleanest available case for the "the dome cannot predict, only relabel" thesis — the code lays it bare.

defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim: Every physical model must adopt some empirical constants (c, G, ℏ). Adopting 24.84 h is no different.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter: c, G, ℏ are fundamental constants that enter theories with stated dimensions and roles. 24.84 h is a DERIVED period whose value depends on Earth's rotation rate, the moon's orbital rate, and their spherical-geometry relationship. Adopting it as a "constant" is like adopting the mass of the Sun as a fundamental constant instead of deriving it from orbital dynamics — category error. The globe derives 24.84 h from ω_Earth and ω_moon. The dome must either do the same derivation (with dome-side rotation and moon-circuit parameters), or admit 24.84 h is observational input rather than prediction.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim_2: The firmament rotates and the moon moves with it — the 24.84 h emerges from their joint motion, even if not spelled out in monitor.py.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter_2: Then the joint-motion derivation should be in the code and should give 24.84 h as an output, not an input. The current code has LUNAR_H as an INPUT on line 218 and uses it as the basis for M2 prediction on line 442. If the derivation existed, the `LUNAR_H` constant would be removed and M2 would be computed directly from moon-circuit parameters — but no such script exists in the repo (verified: a recursive tree listing returns no file computing lunar day from dome geometry; grep for moon-related numerical constants finds only the altitude 2,534 and no circuit rate).

suggested_additions.target: SEC-2.2, append a paragraph after the "timing problem cuts deeper" paragraph
suggested_additions.text: The timing problem is visible in the dome's own verification code. monitor.py (lines 441–452) computes M2 as m2_pred = LUNAR_H / 2 = 24.84/2 = 12.42 h, where the factor of 2 is inherited from Newtonian tidal theory: the tidal potential on a sphere expands as the second-rank Legendre polynomial P₂(cos θ), producing two bulges per lunar day and thus an M2 period equal to half the lunar day. The same divisor appears in the S2 solar-tidal domain (SOLAR_H / 2). The "/2" is the spherical-harmonic fingerprint. The dome's own geometry (moon at 2,534 km over a disc) produces a single-lobed ℓ=0-like tidal force field, not an ℓ=2 field — yet the verification script divides by 2 anyway. A model whose code assumes two bulges and whose geometry produces one spike is contradicting itself, not predicting tides.
suggested_additions.rationale: Converts SEC-2.2 from a purely external (geometry) argument into a self-contradiction argument using the dome's own code. Strengthens the TLDR claim and eliminates the "aetheric tides avoid this" escape by showing the code itself assumes gravitational-sphere tidal theory.

suggested_additions.target: SEC-2.2, add a short "Timing-constant inheritance" note at the end of the "timing problem" paragraph
suggested_additions.text: The lunar-day value 24.84 h, the solar-day 24.00 h, and the sidereal day 23.9345 h are all hardcoded in monitor.py (lines 218–220) under a "LOCKED" banner, with no dome-side derivation. SIDEREAL = 23.9345 h is defined as Earth's rotation relative to the fixed stars — a quantity that requires a sphere rotating in an inertial frame; LUNAR_H = 24.84 h is the synodic combination of SIDEREAL and the moon's 27.32-day sidereal orbital period. The dome has no scripts computing these from disc rotation or moon-circuit parameters; it simply imports the globe's values. The K1 "prediction" (line 457) is explicit: K1 = sidereal_day = 23.9345 h, with predicted = observed and 0.0% error reported. Claiming these as predictive confirmations is a category error — the same number is being written down on both sides of the equation.
suggested_additions.rationale: Extends the one-spike argument to the hour values themselves. A reader who accepts the spatial argument also needs to see the temporal inputs are globe observables, so that the whole tidal pipeline (inputs × formula × comparison) is dome-as-relabeling from end to end.

suggested_additions.target: SEC-2.2 TLDR update (if either suggestion is adopted)
suggested_additions.current: Five tidal WINs (WIN-045/046/049/050/051) all depend on a single geometric constraint: a local moon at 2,534 km over a 20,015 km disc. Working through the geometry, a nearby moon produces a single tidal bulge directly beneath it — not the two-bulge semidiurnal pattern observed globally. The dome would need to explain why opposite sides of the disc rise simultaneously; it doesn't.
suggested_additions.proposed: Five tidal WINs (WIN-045/046/049/050/051) all depend on a single geometric constraint: a local moon at 2,534 km over a 20,015 km disc. The dome's geometry produces a single tidal spike, not the observed two-bulge pattern — and the dome's own verification code (monitor.py) silently assumes two bulges anyway, dividing the lunar day by 2 to match M2. The model is self-contradictory: one-spike geometry with two-bulge arithmetic.
suggested_additions.rationale: Makes the self-contradiction visible to a layman reader; the current TLDR undersells the strength once the fingerprint is added.

files_checked: Cross-search via GitHub code search API
key_findings: monitor.py line 218: LUNAR_H = 24.84 (hardcoded under "LOCKED" banner, no derivation)
monitor.py lines 441–452: M2 domain uses m2_pred = LUNAR_H / 2 with formula string "M2 = lunar_circuit/2 = 24.84/2"
monitor.py lines 454–462: K1 domain explicitly says "K1 = sidereal_day = 23.9345 h" with predicted = SIDEREAL and observed = 23.9345 — tautological 0.0% error
monitor.py lines 588–596: S2 domain uses SOLAR_H / 2 with the same /2 divisor — confirming the pattern is systematic, not isolated
Zero hits for 'amphidromic', 'DOODSON' in the entire repo
Zero hits for '2534' (moon altitude) in tidal-related scripts — moon altitude never enters any tidal computation
No file in the repo derives any tidal period from disc radius, moon altitude, or firmament geometry
The dome's entire tidal "prediction" pipeline is: hardcode globe rotation periods → divide by 2 using globe spherical-harmonic logic → compare to observations → report percent error

newsworthy: Dome's own code assumes two tidal bulges while its geometry produces one spike: M2 = LUNAR_H / 2 in monitor.py imports Newton's sphere-tidal 'divide by 2' despite the model's geometry forbidding it
cross_references: SEC-2.1 fingerprint: similar "LOCKED fit constants masquerading as first-principles" pattern for H(r)
